volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 2.6.514b - Section 2.7.614)

sections.label: Section 2.6.514b
sections.original: Vbi is est , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Prithee , where is he ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.515
sections.original: Mars peregre adveniens salutat Nerienem uxorem suam .
sections.translation: Mars , on his arrival from abroad , salutes Neriene his spouse [*] . Since you ’ ve well got over it , and since you ’ ve been blest with offspring , I congratulate you in that you have given birth to a great glory to me and to yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.6.518
sections.original: Salve , qui me interfecisti paene vita et lumine
sections.translation: Welcome to you , you who have almost deprived me of life and light ;

sections.label: Section 2.6.519
sections.original: quique mihi magni doloris per voluptatem tuam
sections.translation: and who have , for your own gratification , centred in my body the cause of great anguish , with the pangs of which I ’ m even now dreadfully afflicted .

sections.label: Section 2.6.521
sections.original: Heia , haud ab re , mea voluptas , tibi istic obvenit labos :
sections.translation: Well , well ; not to your misfortune , my love , do these pains befall you . You ’ ve brought forth a son who ’ ll be filling your house with plunder .

sections.label: Section 2.6.523
sections.original: Multo ecastor magis oppletis tritici ópust granariis ,
sections.translation: By the powers , there ’ s very much greater need to have our granaries well filled with wheat ; lest , before he takes the plunder , hunger should be putting an end to us here .

sections.label: Section 2.6.525
sections.original: Habe bonum animum .
sections.translation: Be of good heart .

sections.label: Section 2.6.525b
sections.original: Savium pete hinc sis . ah , nequeo caput
sections.translation: Do , please , take a kiss from me here . I cannot lift up my head ; such pain I ’ ve felt , and in such pain I now am ; and I cannot as yet , of my own strength , walk upon my feet .

sections.label: Section 2.6.527b
sections.original: Si hercle me ex medio mari
sections.translation: If , right from the middle of the sea , you were to order me to take a kiss from you , I would not hesitate to fetch it , my sweet . You ’ ve experienced it already so to be ; and now shall you experience it , my Phronesium ,

sections.label: Section 2.6.530
sections.original: me te amare . adduxi ancillas tibi eccas ex Suria duas ,
sections.translation: that I do dote upon you . ( Pointing to a distance behind him .) See there , I ’ ve brought you two female slaves from Syria ; I present you with them . ( To a SERVANT behind him .) Do you bring those women this way . Now , these were both of them queens at their own homes ; but with my own hand I laid waste their country , I present you with them .

sections.label: Section 2.6.533b
sections.original: Paenitetne te quot ancillas alam ,
sections.translation: Are you dissatisfied with the number of female slaves I have already , that you must be still making additions to the number , to be devouring food for me ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.535
sections.original: Hoc quidem herclést ingratum donum . cedo tu mi istam purpuram .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , if this indeed isn ’ t acceptable to you , you boy ( beckoning to the SERVANT ) , do you give me that bag . See here , my love , I ’ ve brought this mantle from Panchaea [*] for you . Take it for yourself . ( He presents it to her .)

sections.label: Section 2.6.537b
sections.original: Hocine mi ob labores tantos tantillum dari ?
sections.translation: What , is so little as this to be given me in return for pain so great ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.538
sections.original: Perii hercle ego miser . iam mi auro contra constat filius :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , to my misfortune , I ’ m undone ! My son ’ s already costing me his weight in gold . ( To PHRONESIUM .) Do you still set such little value on me ? I ’ ve brought you a purple garment from Sarra [*] , and two pretty ones from Pontus . ( Takes the garments from the SERVANT , and presents them .)

sections.label: Section 2.6.541
sections.original: Accipe hoc , Astaphium , abduce hasce hinc e conspectu Suras .
sections.translation: Take this for yourself , my love . ( To the SERVANT .) Take those Syrian women hence out of my sight . ( The SERVANT takes them into the house .)

sections.label: Section 2.6.542
sections.original: Ecquid amas me ?
sections.translation: Do you love me at all ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.542b
sections.original: Nihil ecastor , neque meres .
sections.translation: Not at all , i ’ faith ; nor do you deserve it .

sections.label: Section 2.6.542c
sections.original: Nilne huic sat est ?
sections.translation: What , is nothing enough for her ? Really , to myself she hasn ’ t even said one word . I do believe that these presents would sell for more than twenty minae , which I have given her .

sections.label: Section 2.6.545
sections.original: vehementer nunc mi est irata , sentio atque intellego ;
sections.translation: Now she ’ s desperately enraged with me I perceive and understand it ; but I ’ ll address her still . ( To PHRONESIUM .) What say you then ? Do you wish me , my love , to go to dinner where I was invited , and after that to return hither to your house to sleep ? Why are you silent ? ( Aside .) I ’ troth , beyond a doubt , I ’ m undone . But what fine affair is this ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.549
sections.original: quis hic homost , qui ducit pompam tantam ? certumst quo ferant
sections.translation: Who ’ s this fellow that ’ s leading such a long train ? I ’ m resolved to watch whither they are taking it . It ’ s being brought to her , I do believe ; but I ’ ll soon know more .

sections.label: Section 2.7.551
sections.original: Ite íte hac simúl , mulierí damnigéruli ,
sections.translation: Get on , get on this way together with you , mules laden with money only to be squandered , you emptyers out of the house , you carriers off of property by waggon-loads ! ( To the AUDIENCE .) And can ’ t he who is in love do without being good for nought , and cleaning himself out by his disgraceful practices ? But how I know this , don ’ t any one be asking that of me ;

sections.label: Section 2.7.555
sections.original: domist qui facit improba facta amator ,
sections.translation: we ’ ve a lover at home , who ’ s engaged in disgraceful pursuits ; who esteems property just as dung : he ’ s in dread of the public officers [*] ; most cleanly in his ways is he . He wishes his house to be cleaned out ; whatever he has at home , it ’ s swept completely dehors [*] . Since he himself is sending his own self to ruin , for my part , i ’ faith , I ’ ll help him by stealth ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.560
sections.original: néque mea quidem ópera umquam hilo mínus propere quam póte peribit .
sections.translation: and not through my assistance , indeed , shall he be ruined e ’ en a bit the less speedily than he may . For now from these provisions , from the one mina I ’ ve just now abstracted five didrachms ; I ’ ve deducted for myself the Herculean share [*] . But this is just like as though a person should turn off a stream for himself from a river ; if it is not turned off into a channel , still all that water would go into the sea .

sections.label: Section 2.7.565
sections.original: nam hoc in mare abit misereque perit sine bona omni gratia .
sections.translation: For this is going into the sea , and is being utterly wasted to no good purpose whatever . When I see these things going on , I pilfer , I purloin , from plunder I plunder take . I take a harlot to be just like what the sea is ; what you give her she swallows down , and yet never overflows .

sections.label: Section 2.7.570
sections.original: hoc sáltem : rem sérvat nec úlli ubi sit appáret :
sections.translation: But this at least the sea does preserve ; what ’ s in it is seen . Give her as much as ever you please , it ’ s never seen either by the giver or the acceptor . For instance , this harlot by her blandishments has reduced my poor master to poverty ; has robbed him of fortune , life , honor , and friends .

sections.label: Section 2.7.575
sections.original: áttat , eccam adest propinque . credo audisse haec me loqui .
sections.translation: ( Catching sight of her .) Heyday ! why look , she ’ s close by . I do think she has heard me saying all this . She ’ s pale , as she has been delivered of a child . I ’ ll address her , as though I didn ’ t know her . ( Addressing PHRONESIUM .) I present you my respects .

sections.label: Section 2.7.577b
sections.original: Noster Cyame , quid agis ? ut vales ?
sections.translation: Our Geta , what is it you ’ re about ? How are you ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.578
sections.original: Valeo , et venio ad minus valentem , et melius qui valeat fero .
sections.translation: I ’ m well , and I ’ m come to one who ’ s not so well , and I ’ m bringing something with which she may get well . My master , the apple of your eye , bade me bring these presents to you ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.580
sections.original: dona quae vides íllos ferre , et has quinque argenti minas .
sections.translation: which you see those persons carrying , and these five minae of silver .

sections.label: Section 2.7.581
sections.original: Pol haúd perit quod illum tántum amo .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , it ’ s not thrown away , that I ’ m so very fond of him .

sections.label: Section 2.7.582
sections.original: Iússit oráre , ut haec gráta haberés tibi .
sections.translation: He bade me beg that you would accept these in kind part .

sections.label: Section 2.7.583
sections.original: Grata ácceptaque ecastór habeo . iube vása auferri intro , i Cyame .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , I do accept them kindly and thankfully . ( To CYAMUS , who comes out of the house .) Go , Cyamus , and order them to be taken in-doors .

sections.label: Section 2.7.584
sections.original: Écquid audítis , haec quaé iam Cyamo ímperat ?
sections.translation: Do you hear at all this which has been ordered ? ( The SERVANTS take up the things .)

sections.label: Section 2.7.585
sections.original: Vása nolo aúferant : désiccarí iube .
sections.translation: I don ’ t want them to take away the vessels ; I ’ d like to have them emptied .

sections.label: Section 2.7.586
sections.original: Ímpudens mecástor , Cyame , es .
sections.translation: An impudent fellow , i ’ faith , how busy he is .

sections.label: Section 2.7.586d
sections.original: Boná fide ?
sections.translation: What ? do you say that I ’ m impudent , you who yourself are a receptacle of villany ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.588
sections.original: Díc , amabó te , ubi est Díniarchús ?
sections.translation: Tell me , I beg of you , where is Dinarchus ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.588b
sections.original: Domi .
sections.translation: At home .

sections.label: Section 2.7.589
sections.original: Díc ob haec dóna , quae ad mé hodie míserit ,
sections.translation: Tell him , that , for these presents which he has sent me ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.590
sections.original: me íllum amare plurimum ómnium hominum merito ,
sections.translation: I love him the most of all men , and that in return I hold him in the highest esteem of all , and entreat that he will come hither to me .

sections.label: Section 2.7.592b
sections.original: Ílicet .
sections.translation: This instant . But who ’ s that person , pray ( looking towards STRATOPHANES ) , that ’ s devouring himself [*] , scowling with malignant eyes I ’ faith , the man ’ s distressed in his mind ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.595
sections.original: quísquis est .
sections.translation: whoever he is .

sections.label: Section 2.7.595b
sections.original: Dignúst mecastor . néquam est . non nosti , óbsecro ,
sections.translation: I ’ troth , he ’ s deserving of it .

sections.label: Section 2.7.596
sections.original: militem , hic apud me quí erat ? huius pater pueri illic est .
sections.translation: Prithee , don ’ t you know ? [*] He that ’ s yonder used to live with me ; he , there , is the father of my child . He ordered it to be brought up till about a before-daylight breakfast [*] . I waited his coming , I listened to his injunctions ; I attended to the child .

sections.label: Section 2.7.598b
sections.original: Novi hóminem nihili .
sections.translation: The good-for-nothing fellow that I knew so well , prithee , is that he ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.599b
sections.original: Íllic est .
sections.translation: That ’ s he .

sections.label: Section 2.7.599c
sections.original: Me íntuetúr gemens ;
sections.translation: He looks at me as he groans .

sections.label: Section 2.7.600
sections.original: traxit ex intimo ventre suspiritum .
sections.translation: He heaved a sigh from the very bottom of his breast . Observe that now ; he gnashes his teeth ; he strikes his thigh . Prithee , is he deranged [*] , that he ’ s beating his own self ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.603
sections.original: Nunc égo meos animos víolentos meamque íram ex pectore iám promam .
sections.translation: Now will I at once summon up my ungovernable indignation and my wrath from my breast !

sections.label: Section 2.7.604_605
sections.original: loquere : únde es ? cuius es ? cúr ausu'sausus es mi inclémenter dicére ?
sections.translation: ( To GETA .) Say , whence are you ? To whom do you belong ? Why have you dared to speak rudely against me ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.604_605b
sections.original: Lubitumst .
sections.translation: It ’ s my pleasure .

sections.label: Section 2.7.606
sections.original: Istúcine mihi respóndes ?
sections.translation: Do you answer me in that way ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.606b
sections.original: Hoc . non égo te flocci fácio .
sections.translation: In this way ( snaps his fingers ) ; I don ’ t care one straw for you .

sections.label: Section 2.7.607
sections.original: Quid tú ? cur ausa es álium te dicére amare hominem ?
sections.translation: What say you ? Why have you dared to say that you love another man ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.607b
sections.original: Lúbitumst .
sections.translation: I chose to .

sections.label: Section 2.7.608
sections.original: Ain tándem ? istuc primum éxperiar . tun tántilli doní causa ,
sections.translation: Say you so , indeed ? I ’ ll first make trial of that . Do you , for the sake of such a shabby present ,

sections.label: Section 2.7.609_610
sections.original: holerum átque escarum et póscarum , moechúm malacum , cincínnatum ,
sections.translation: vegetables , and comestibles , and vinegar-water , bestow your love upon an effeminate , frizzle-pated , dark-haunt frequenting , drum-drubbing debauchee [*] , a fellow not worth a nutshell ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.611b
sections.original: Quae haéc res ?
sections.translation: What new thing ’ s this ? Do you dare , you rogue , to speak ill of my master , you spring-head of vice and perjury ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.613
sections.original: Verbum únum adde istoc : iam hércle ego te hic hac óffatim confíciam .
sections.translation: Add a single word to that ; by the powers , I ’ ll that instant here with this cut you up into mincemeat upon the spot .

sections.label: Section 2.7.614
sections.original: Tangé modo , iám ego te hic agnum faciam ét medium distrúncabo .
sections.translation: Only touch me ; I ’ ll that instant be making a lamb of you on the spot , and I ’ ll slice you asunder in the middle .
